data.text:
INT: Tape five of Mark Verstandig. Yes, you're telling us about yeshiva. You started yeshiva. How old were you? 

MV: Before I started yeshiva, you see, my mother wanted me to become a rabbi. And she could see that I'm not going to become a rabbi from the Talmud Torah. So we had-- she-- we had a relative who was a rabbi himself, but he didn't have a job. He was not-- he was unemployed. 

He-- he was from another town, from Sokolow. So my mother arranged that he use to-- that he came to live with us on his own. And he became my full-time teacher, from the morning till the night. He was to be maybe at least two-- [NON-ENGLISH], which means two seasons. Two six-month seasons he was there. 

He was a very-- he was a very intelligent man, and a great scholar. And he understood psychology, the psychology of children. He knew that a child 12 years old or 10 years old is not interested in legal-- in-- in-- in legal arguments of the Talmud. You see, if a-- if an ox bulls a cow, who is responsible, and so on. Along-- this is the Talmud. And he understood it quite-- quite-- quite well. 

Now, he used to-- he used to start in the morning with the Talmud, with a page of-- of the Gemara. But later, after-- after a quarter of an hour, half an hour, he said, look-- look, Mordec, in the afternoon we will be reading the scripture. We will reading the Bible. A really good story about spies and trumpets, how the Jews conquered the city. 

And that I was looking for, the story-- the story of Joshua and so on. And in the afternoon, he picked out stories in the Bible which could interest-- which could interest a child. The-- the wars of Sisera, and, you know, how-- all kind of wars and so on. And he understood that, and that gives-- gave the child some incentive to be attentive in the morning for the Gemara. 

But after a year, he went away. He got a job after he went away. And my mother took me to a yeshiva. To the so-called [NON-ENGLISH]-- the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi-- the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the yeshiva of the-- of the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi. 

And I will never forget the introduction. My mother came with me, holding me. Said to the Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH], said, this is my [NON-ENGLISH], my-- or my youngest son. For nine years, I waited for a son that he should become a rabbi. I will pay you with golden coins. But see to it that he should learn and know the Talmud at least as your Yankele. 

His son Yankele was 16 years old, and he was a genius. People knew he was-- if it wouldn't be a war, he would've been-- he-- he would've been a Mendelssohn. He was really a genius. In 16 years, he had-- he knew the Talmud back and forth. If you-- if you-- if you told-- if you told him a-- a-- a sentence from Talmud, he will tell you this is in volume so-and-so, page so-and-so, three lines from the bottom. He had a photographic memory. 

And making some notations from the Talmud to-- by-- by-- by-- by 16 year, was a genius. And here comes my mother and she thinks that with gold coins, here I would learn-- I would soon know at least as much as-- as-- as his son. And I became the laughing stock of the whole town. That was the problem, the laughing stock. Everybody thinks that this is the man-- that this is the youngster, at least as Yankele. 

INT: How old were you when you started yeshiva? 

MV: I was at least 12 years old. 12, 13. Maybe 13. 12 years, 13 years. 13 years. Would be 13 years. So that was my yeshiva, my-- my yeshiva. 

INT: Was that in Mielec? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: Was that in Mielec? 

MV: In Mielec. Mielec. That was in Mielec. That was around a year-- was-- he was real entitled to become the rabbi of Mielec. Because there were a few brothers, and he was the oldest. And-- but he married a daughter of a rabbi in another town. And this rabbi unusually was quite a wealthy person. So he went over there to [POLISH] to live with his parents-in-law-- with his parents-in-law. 

But later when the parents-in-law died, he came back. But in the meantime, his younger son, who was a fool, became the rabbi. And he couldn't unseat him because he was already employed. So the only thing he could-- he could have done is to open a yeshiva. So I was there in-- in-- in his yeshiva. 

And one-- but I was not very happy. I wanted-- I-- really after-- when I was 14-- 14 years, I decided that-- that enough is enough, that I want to-- not to become a rabbi. Matter of fact, I have to tell you a story about it. 

It was usual that on Shabbat, you should visit some dignitaries in town. A dignitary in town means a Jew who knows the Talmud. And you used to come to him in the afternoon and he used to show off-- he examined you. Well, you should go through a page of Gemara and read it and explain it, how it-- what it means and so on. 

So there were two such people, two such learned people. I used to go every Shabbat, once-- every-- a fortnight. One Shabbat to [NON-ENGLISH], and another Shabbat to [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH], people knew about him. He was the son-in-law of a wealthy family. But everybody knew that he is a highly educated man. He was orthodox. 

But he didn't wear a shtreimel, but a black-- a black hat. He used to go-- he didn't have a long beard, but a very short one. But people knew that he's a highly educated man. And I used to go to him every fortnight. 

And one day, I was certainly 14 years old. One day after-- in the middle-- in the middle of my page, he said to me, tell me, Motel, what books did you read? What books have you read this week? I said, my god, how does he know that I'm reading books? 

But then I realized that he already knows. I said to him, what do you mean? He said, books, books, from libraries, from the Jewish library. Said, my god, he knows already? So if he knows, I may-- I may confess. 

So I told him I read a book which is called The Hero in Chains. He said, a book Hero in Chains? Who is the author of this book? I-- I told him Victor Hugo. He said, it's-- first of all, it's not Victor Hugo, but Victor Ego. Said, look, I read the Hugo, and he tells Hugo-- Ego. He means some-- somebody else. Said, no, it is Hugo. 

What is it? Who is it-- I've never heard of such a title. He said, who is the hero? I said, Jean Valjean. Ah, he said. Went to the other room, took out from his books-- bookshelves a book and showed me the book. And I read it. So I've got the French title. I read it, Les Miserables. I said-- he said it's not Les Miserables, it is Lay Miserable. 

And I said, look, if Victor Hugo is Victor Ego, why shouldn't it be Les Miserables, Lay Miserable. But I-- I am not in a position to argue with this fellow. Anyhow, he was very pleased with me. He was very pleased. And we didn't even finish that page of Gemara, but he was was an-- he's still an orthodox Jew. He stopped to say milah prayer which you-- you have to say before you have shaleshudes, before the third meal on Shabbat. So he stopped to say milah. 

He gave me-- he-- I had to-- he gave me a few-- a few things to eat. I mean, I have to join his-- his shaleshudes. And later he-- he went with me to the door. And I told him, look, I will tell you about your Victor Ego. That the Yiddish translator who translated your Lay Miserable has Hero in Chains understood better the-- the-- this Jean Valjean than Victor Hugo. 

Now he smiles. I just knew. He smiles. He said, who is hero? Why is the hero in chains? Who is the hero in chains? I told him, Jean Valjean. He is in chains of cruel-- old-fashioned cruel laws which doesn't give him a chance to-- to-- to have a-- doesn't-- doesn't give him a break. Doesn't give him a chance to rehabilitate. 

He looks at me. He said, Mordec, I'll tell you something. You will be a good lawyer. Believe me, it's a better-- it's a better job than to be a rabbi in a Jewish community. Remember what I have told you. Be a lawyer. 

This what I am-- and then I decided one day it's enough enough. I have to go-- I have to go to the-- to high school in order to start-- in order to start as a lawyer later. But from-- from that time, he gave me the idea. From that time I thought, really, it's better to be a lawyer than a rabbi. 

INT: So how did you end up starting at the gymnasium? 

MV: I end up or I-- I started? 

INT: Started. 

MV: I started. I started by entering a-- I had to enter a-- an-- an examination. Because I didn't have behind me the-- the schooling, the primary schooling. So I entered into the fifth-- you call it grade-- form or grade? The fifth form. The fifth form. 

My instructor, tutor, wanted me to work with-- he wanted to work with me for another few months that I could go to the seventh form, because I was not ready to the seventh form after a few months tuition. But I was afraid that, in the meantime, my mother, my father, something will-- will go wrong. Because I told them already, look, whatever it is, I'm going. I'm going to-- I'm going to the-- to the gymnasium. 

So I said I would rather go now to the fifth than to wait another few months, which means a year, and to go to the seventh. So I passed the exam. I remember there were two-- there were two-- there was a music teacher, who tried to-- who tried-- I didn't-- I-- I didn't have-- I didn't know music and I didn't know notes. And I don't know up till now to read music. 

And he started with me with do-re-mi and so on, and then said, look, you haven't got-- you haven't got an ear. I said, look, I have. You haven't gotten an ear. You'll-- you'll not-- but that doesn't matter, he said. This subject is no more at the end of this year in-- in the-- in the form, in the sixth form. So I will let you through, because I heard that you are all right in the other-- with-- with the other subjects. 

And so I went to gymnasium. And they didn't have a lot of [NON-ENGLISH] for me. I had a very interesting case over there. As a matter of fact, Paul Barber picked up this from my book, the question of-- of a-- of a-- of a professor so-called Kozak. He taught me to tell him-- to tell him this story. 

There was a teacher. All teachers in gymnasium were called professors. There was a fellow-- a fellow called Kozak. Everybody knew-- everybody knew that he is a very difficult person. And the moment I came in, or he became-- he came to my class, it was the sixth class already. He came to the class. And he wanted the children to introduce themselves. 

So he started to read out the children from the book. Everybody had to stand up, and he called his name and he looked at him. Stand up. And it came to me, at my name. I stood up. And he looked at me and looked and looked, and didn't go further. I'm sure that he's-- he-- he's asleep with open eyes. Didn't say a word. 

I knew already at that time that he had some-- my-- that he had some trouble with my brother. And in the end, my brother made-- my father made a complaint. But it didn't succeed and my brother had to leave this gymnasium, go to another town because of him. So he looks at me, looks at me. 

You know, OK. Few days later-- for a few days he was lecturing a certain period of law in the-- or they call-- before the Middle Ages. How you call this period, [NON-ENGLISH]? The period of-- the Roman period of history. 

He was-- he was lecturing about this Roman period of history, from [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was-- was the historian who wrote-- who was the author of this school-- school text-- of the textbook. And then a few days later, he asked me a question. He-- he-- he asked me. This was the way of examining children in-- in Poland. 

You used to call them up and ask him about all these lectures, about all this period he was lecturing about. I told him everything. Told him more than it-- than it was in the-- in-- in-- in the textbook, in-- in [NON-ENGLISH] textbook. He listened, listened, listened. And then he said to me, sit down, you idiot. 

I sat down. A moment later, I jumped up. I said, look, if I am an idiot, so is [NON-ENGLISH] an idiot. And so are all people who are relying on [NON-ENGLISH] are idiots. He jumped down from this place to me. I was in the first row. He grabbed me like that at the collar and dragged me to the door, opened the door, and pushed me out. 

I was standing outside walking around the corridor. In the-- in the meantime, the headmaster went by. Asked me, what are you doing here? I told him the story. And he looks at me and said, I will look at-- I-- I will look at this-- at this matter. And after the hour, I went back to school. But when I went home, I knew already that my schooling in Mielec is finished like it was with my brother. 

Next day in the morning, I went with a heavy heart to school. I knew that it's my last day, that I will be thrown out. And then when it came the time for Kozak's-- Kozak's hour, another teacher came. Another professor came in and said that Professor Kozak is not well. So he will be in the meantime. 

A-- a couple of hours later, by the-- before the end of-- before the end of the-- before 1 o'clock-- the school only goes from 8:00 to 1:00-- everybody already knew that during the night, Kozak has a break down. They had to take him to an asylum, to a-- he was-- he-- he became just insane. He was-- he was insane. So he-- they locked him up in a-- they-- they-- they locked him up. That was the end of this period. 

Oh, that was that. But with the other teachers, I had a very nice-- very-- very nice relationship. There was a teacher, a Professor [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was not an antisemite. And he-- but he was a little meshugga just the same. 

His method of examining the children was similar to Kozak's. He used to go-- but he used to go on for a couple of weeks, for three weeks sometimes lecturing history. Later his meshuggas was that he used to come open his notebook and read from the notebook the names of the-- of the pupils, from A to Z. And he asked a question. If you didn't start with the right word as he imagined that you should start, he used to say [POLISH] and put in a form, which means the-- you didn't pass the exam today. 

And so it was. I was on V. There's a long while-- a long while to go. And it took about half an hour until he came to me. All other people were-- were already [POLISH]. And the question was, I remember, the-- the-- the history of the Reformation. 

I said, he's not going to tell me [POLISH]. And it came to me. I started. There is a a mime comedy reflecting the history of the Reformation. He looked up. And I told him scene after scene of this mime comedy. And I-- I still remember the last-- the last scene. The last scene was that the emperor comes up to the stage. There is a fire on the stage. The emperor comes up and he want-- he wants to extinguish the fire. 

There was a barrel at the corner. And he grabs the barrel, pours the barrel on the fire. But-- but there was no water. It was oil, and the whole scene is full of oil-- is full of fire. And I told him this comedy was written by that-- that-- there-- there-- there-- there are-- they-- they supposed that the author of this comedy was Erasmus from Rotterdam. And it's written in Latin. 

He looked at me. He came back to me. Put hand to shoulder. It's a privilege to have a pupil like you, just a privilege. Look at him, he said. And next day-- next day he brought me essays-- historic-- essays of history, and to-- told him-- he used to-- he used to meet my father because they were councilors in the city council. He said, look, your son will be a great scholar, and so on and so on. 

Until-- until-- until-- until he had an idea that I am a communist. The reason was that I was-- I befriended-- there was a-- a friend of a-- of a-- of a pupil called Zbreschavski, [POLISH] Andrei Zbreschavski. He was a-- a-- a highly skilled mathematician. A brilliant mathematician and a very intelligent fellow. 

And then-- you see-- and I-- in the meantime I organized the Betar. And he used to come. He was a good-- a personal friend of mine. We used to spend hours reading and so on. So I used to-- he used to come with me to the Betar. And he used to dance the horah. He used to sit and listen to the conversations, to the [YIDDISH]. This is the-- this is the conversations about Jewish history, about Jewish problems, and so on. He used to sit down together with us. 

What we didn't know was that the Polish government was suspicious of Jewish organizations, especially of Jewish youngsters' organizations, thinking that they are communist organizations. 

So they were spying on them. Now, what would Andrei [POLISH] Zbreschavski-- top from the gentry-- Andrei [POLISH] Zbreschavski do in the Betar? 

In a Jewish Zionist organization? It must be a communist organization, which Andrei Zbreschavski interest in. They are running a communist organization. And he came to my father-- the professor came to my father and told me, look, I've got all information about your son. It is not a Betar. It is not a Zionist. He is a communist. And look, look around and you'll see that every [YIDDISH]-- he used the expression-- [YIDDISH] is a servant. 

Every [YIDDISH] is his colleague, is his friend. He doesn't-- he-- he doesn't push away that-- and that shows you that he and Andrei Zbreschavski and so on, they all are communists. And that was the end of this-- of this nice relationship between me and [NON-ENGLISH]. But still later he realized that he made a mistake. He went to see-- in Borowa he went to see his-- Zbreschavski's father who was a doctor. And he was an invalid doctor. 

And he was-- and he-- he really saw-- he really-- he-- he realized that he made a mistake, that I am not a communist. And-- but I'm-- I wasn't sure about it until one day, there was an-- an assignment to write about the-- the history of privileges in Poland. You know, the privileges of the classes of this-- in-- in Poland. You had to make a-- and it's an assignment. It would have been enough, a couple of pages. 

I wrote-- I finished a whole exercise book from A to Z on the principles of Marx, just on the principles of Marx. It's a class war. You know, from A to Z, how they extracted-- the privileges were not given, but they were extracted by classes. The class war, and so on and so on. And I gave all the kind-- all kind of-- of Marx-- of Marx-- in the-- teaching in this exercise book. 

At the end, I-- I wrote, 'If required, I will write again this whole essay proving that Marx was wrong.' That this has nothing to do with-- that-- that it was-- it was-- it was a fallacy from A to Z. Yeah. I got an-- I got a note, 'Excellent. I don't need-- we-- we don't-- we know-- we know it is wrong, but for the serious approach it is an excellent essay.' 

INT: How old were you when you started gymnasium? 

MV: How old I was? Must have been 15 years, because I finished by the-- when I was 19. 

INT: And you were five years in gymnasium? 

MV: Yeah, five year. Five, six, seven, eight. Four years. 

INT: Four years in gymnasium. 

MV: Four years in gymnasium. So I started when I was 15. And I-- yes, I started when I was 15. And I finished when I was 19. 

INT: What was your command of Polish like when you started-- 

MV: Oh, excellent. I knew Polish very well. I knew Polish very well from the-- from-- from-- before entering. Before-- before entering this, because I was reading a lot of Polish literature before I entered, before I went there. And besides this, I-- I-- I was reading the papers. I-- my command of Polish was perfect. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue in the next tape.
